question: Is the 24-month refueling cycle (Ch.01) consistent with the Ch.05 RCS design for cooldown and depressurization?

nuscale_ch01_0036.title: Refueling Operations
nuscale_ch01_0036.summary: Refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM) are conducted independently from other modules. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, disassembling it for core refueling and maintenance, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing and inspection.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the applicable material specifications for components such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting.

nuscale_ch01_0439.title: SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3: Condensate Cleanup SystemII.2 Maintain indirect cycle PWR water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)
nuscale_ch01_0439.summary: The section outlines NuScale’s compliance with regulatory criteria for maintaining indirect cycle PWR water quality to prevent corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, as required by GDC 14. It notes that in the NuScale design, primary water is outside the steam generator tubes and secondary water is inside, with no steam generator blowdown, resulting in secondary chemistry requirements that differ from standard guidelines.

nuscale_ch01_0309.title: SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4: Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity DesignII.3 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 61
nuscale_ch01_0309.summary: This section documents NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity Design, specifically addressing compliance with General Design Criterion (GDC) 61. It confirms that the design meets the required safety standards outlined in SRP 9.1.4, with no exceptions or additional comments noted.

nuscale_ch01_0438.title: SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3: Condensate Cleanup SystemII.1 Maintain direct cycle BWR plant water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)
nuscale_ch01_0438.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for various systems, noting that certain requirements are not applicable due to design differences (e.g., BWR-only criteria or absence of Class 2/3 components). For the Condensate Cleanup System, NuScale confirms that BWR-specific water quality requirements do not apply, but for PWR systems, their design meets corrosion prevention standards, with unique secondary chemistry requirements due to the steam generator configuration.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five, which covers the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It specifies that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the information for regulatory purposes, and any copies must include the appropriate copyright and proprietary notices.

nuscale_ch01_0862.title: Table 1.9-4: Conformance with Interim Staff Guidance (ISG)
nuscale_ch01_0862.summary: Table 1.9-4 summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) sections, particularly regarding seismic issues and other regulatory criteria. The table indicates that NuScale’s design generally conforms to seismic-related ISGs, with some partial conformance where site-specific information is required, and notes that certain ISGs are not applicable to the current application stage.

nuscale_ch01_0662.title: DSRS 15.0.3, Rev 0: Design Basis Accident Radiological Consequence Analyses for the NuScale SMR DesignII.2 Control Room Radiological Habitability
nuscale_ch01_0662.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale SMR design’s conformance with regulatory criteria for radiological consequence analyses during design basis accidents, specifically addressing control room radiological habitability. It confirms that the NuScale design meets the relevant requirements for protecting control room personnel from radiological exposure during postulated accidents, as outlined in DSRS 15.0.3 and related sections. The document also notes partial conformance for the Technical Support Center, with full criteria met when AC power is available.

nuscale_ch01_0130.title: 1.9.5 Advanced and Evolutionary Light-Water Reactor Design Issues
nuscale_ch01_0130.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report aligns with regulatory criteria, incorporating relevant operational experience and NRC guidance into the reactor design. It details the evaluation process for supplier compliance, including reviews of 10 CFR 21 notifications, and confirms that all applicable notifications have been addressed. Additionally, it references NRC policy and licensing issues for advanced and evolutionary light-water reactor designs, as outlined in SECY-93-087 and related guidance.

nuscale_ch01_0813.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.7 Criteria to determine the acceptability of the D&S System compliance with the requirements of GDC 20
nuscale_ch01_0813.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or detect-and-suppress methods for thermal hydraulic stability; instead, existing reactor trip signals act preemptively to prevent conditions that could lead to flow instabilities or SAFDL violations. While the system partially conforms to GDC 20 requirements by tripping the reactor before instability can develop, it does not actively detect or suppress instabilities, and backup options are not required due to the inherent design features.

nuscale_ch01_0814.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.8 Detect and Suppress system to monitor process variables and systems.
nuscale_ch01_0814.summary: The NuScale design does not use a Detect and Suppress (D&S) system to monitor process variables for thermal hydraulic stability, as reactor trip signals are designed to act before unstable flow oscillations can develop. Therefore, the D&S system is deemed not applicable, and stability is managed through existing reactor trip systems rather than by detecting and suppressing instabilities.

nuscale_ch01_0663.title: DSRS 15.0.3, Rev 0: Design Basis Accident Radiological Consequence Analyses for the NuScale SMR DesignII.3 Technical Support Center Radiological Habitability
nuscale_ch01_0663.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for radiological consequence analyses during design basis accidents, specifically addressing the Technical Support Center (TSC) radiological habitability. It notes that the TSC meets dose acceptance criteria when AC power is available, but its function is transferred to the main control room if AC power is lost, indicating only partial conformance for this aspect.

nuscale_ch01_0387.title: SRP 9.5.1.1, Rev 0: Fire Protection ProgramII.4 Fire Protection for Permanently Shutdown and Decommissioning Reactor Plants
nuscale_ch01_0387.summary: Section SRP 9.5.1.1, Rev 0: Fire Protection for Permanently Shutdown and Decommissioning Reactor Plants is not applicable to the NuScale design, as it only applies to reactor licensees that have submitted certifications for license termination under 10 CFR 50.82. Therefore, NuScale does not address this criterion in its safety analysis report.

nuscale_ch05_0018.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0018.summary: This section outlines the inspection, shipment, installation, operating conditions, inservice surveillance, and threaded fastener requirements for the NuScale reactor vessel, referencing relevant ASME and ASTM standards. It specifies procedures for maintaining vessel integrity during transport and operation, and lists the key codes and standards used for compliance.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s passive safety features, including two independent decay heat removal trains, each capable of fully cooling the reactor core using natural circulation and passive condensers. In the event of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, the system isolates the affected components and relies on heat transfer to a large, stainless steel-lined reactor pool (the ultimate heat sink) to safely dissipate decay heat, ensuring continued cooling without active power sources.

nuscale_ch01_0037.title: Control Building
nuscale_ch01_0037.summary: This section describes the refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM), which are conducted independently for each module. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, disassembling it for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing and inspection.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the methodology and regulatory compliance for establishing pressure-temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes how these limits are calculated using ASME and NRC guidelines, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the resulting curves and data to ensure protection against non-ductile failure during various operational conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0659.title: SRP 15.0.2, (March 2007): Review of Transient and Accident Analysis MethodsII.4 Uncertainty Analysis
nuscale_ch01_0659.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analysis methods, noting partial or non-applicability where NuScale’s design or methodologies differ from traditional requirements (such as using CHF instead of DNBR, and a modified AST methodology). It also highlights that NuScale’s evaluation models for loss-of-coolant accidents (LOCAs) address only the technically relevant features required by regulations, supporting exemptions from certain portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K.

nuscale_ch01_0657.title: SRP 15.0.2, (March 2007): Review of Transient and Accident Analysis MethodsII.2 Accident Scenario Identification Process
nuscale_ch01_0657.summary: This section outlines NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analysis methods, specifically referencing SRP 15.0.2 (March 2007). It states that NuScale’s accident scenario identification process fully conforms to the guidance, while other areas either partially conform or are not applicable due to design differences or use of alternative methodologies. The NuScale design also supports exemptions from certain regulatory requirements, focusing only on technically relevant features for its evaluation models.

nuscale_ch01_0811.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.5 Detect and Suppress Method Trip of reactor before SAFDL violation
nuscale_ch01_0811.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or detect and suppress (D&S) methods for thermal hydraulic stability; instead, existing reactor trip signals act preemptively to prevent violation of safety limits before unstable flow oscillations can develop. The system partially conforms to some regulatory criteria by ensuring reactor trips occur before safety limits are challenged, but does not employ dedicated D&S systems or backup options, as these are deemed unnecessary due to the design’s inherent protections.

nuscale_ch01_0035.title: Fuel Handling and Reactor Maintenance Areas
nuscale_ch01_0035.summary: The fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas are located at the west end of the Reactor Building and include spaces for the spent fuel pool, refueling pool, and dry dock, all designed to facilitate safe fuel handling and reactor maintenance operations. These areas provide equipment and access for refueling, fuel assembly transport, and storage, while ensuring radiation protection for personnel.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and design requirements for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspection and testing of reactor coolant pressure boundary components are conducted according to the latest applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) standards, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

nuscale_ch01_0810.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.4 Detect and Suppress Method: Exclusion zone and buffer region methodology
nuscale_ch01_0810.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability, as reactor trip signals are designed to prevent conditions that could lead to unstable flow oscillations. Instead, the existing reactor trip system (RTS) provides protection by tripping the reactor before safety limits are violated, making additional detect and suppress (D&S) methods unnecessary. As a result, backup D&S options and related instrumentation are not applicable, and the system partially conforms to criteria requiring detection and suppression of instabilities.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance statements relevant to the RCS design, while Section B is the preface to the chapter detailing the RCS design, indicating that Section A's regulatory context applies to the content introduced in Section B.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance related to refueling and spent fuel handling, which are operationally dependent on the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) design described in Section B, thus connecting the refueling cycle duration to the technical capabilities of the RCS.

Section B provides detailed specifications of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressurizer design, which Section A references as part of the safety analysis to ensure that the RCS can support operational requirements such as the 24-month refueling cycle.

Section A establishes that the NuScale design incorporates relevant operational experience and regulatory guidance, which underpins the refueling operations process described in Section B, ensuring that the 24-month refueling cycle is supported by proven and compliant design practices.

Section A discusses the incorporation of operational experience and regulatory guidance into the NuScale design, while Section B provides specific material and component details for the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressurizer, with Section A's general regulatory conformance being specified by the detailed design information in Section B.

Section A describes the incorporation of operational experience and regulatory guidance into the NuScale design, while Section B lists specific SRP criteria and their conformance, connecting them through the regulatory requirements that the design must satisfy.

Section A outlines the thermal hydraulic stability and safety criteria (including cooldown and depressurization requirements) that the RCS design must meet, while Section B describes the operational steps and independence of refueling, connecting them through the need for the RCS design to support safe cooldown and depressurization during the 24-month refueling cycle.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and protective actions for thermal hydraulic stability, which must be satisfied by the RCS design details and materials described in Section B to ensure the 24-month refueling cycle is supported by adequate cooldown and depressurization capabilities.

Section A discusses the thermal hydraulic stability and protective actions (such as reactor trip signals) that ensure safe operation and prevent violation of critical heat flux limits, which supports the feasibility of a 24-month refueling cycle described in Section B by ensuring the RCS design can maintain safe cooldown and depressurization during extended operation.

Section A discusses the thermal hydraulic stability and protective actions in the NuScale design, which are relevant to ensuring that the RCS (described in Section B) can safely accommodate operational cycles such as the 24-month refueling interval by preventing conditions (e.g., unstable flow, CHF violations) that could challenge RCS cooldown and depressurization capabilities.

Section A discusses the detection and suppression of thermal hydraulic instabilities in the RCS, which is necessary to ensure safe cooldown and depressurization, while Section B outlines requirements for maintaining water quality and material integrity in the RCS to prevent corrosion and failure, both of which are prerequisites for supporting a 24-month refueling cycle by ensuring system reliability and safety.

Section A discusses the detection and suppression of thermal hydraulic instabilities to prevent conditions that could challenge RCS cooldown and depressurization, while Section B addresses the radiological consequences of design basis accidents, both of which are connected through their roles in ensuring the safety and regulatory compliance of the RCS design during operational cycles such as the 24-month refueling interval.

Section A discusses radiological consequence analyses for design basis accidents, while Section B clarifies that the NuScale design does not rely on engineered safety feature (ESF) ventilation systems for accident mitigation, indicating that the radiological analyses in Section A are based on the use of nonsafety-related ventilation as described in Section B.

Section A describes inspection and handling requirements for the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for maintaining water quality to prevent corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, connecting them through the shared objective of ensuring RCS integrity over the refueling cycle.

Section A describes the RCS cooldown and decay heat removal capabilities, which are necessary to safely achieve the conditions required for refueling operations as outlined in Section B, thus connecting the technical design to the operational refueling cycle.

Section B provides detailed material specifications for pressurizer components that are part of the RCS design referenced in Section A's description of system operation during cooldown and depressurization.

Section B describes the operational process and frequency of refueling (every 24 months), while Section A details the reactor vessel's compliance with ASME code requirements for structural integrity during cooldown and depressurization, which are necessary to ensure that the vessel can safely withstand the thermal and pressure transients associated with each refueling cycle.

Section A provides detailed ASME code compliance information for reactor vessel attachments, while Section B references the materials and design details of pressurizer components, with both sections connected through their discussion of RPV component materials and compliance with ASME BPVC requirements.

Section A provides technical details and compliance information about reactor vessel materials and welds, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for maintaining water quality to prevent corrosion of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, connecting them through the shared goal of ensuring RCS integrity over the refueling cycle.

Section A provides technical details on reactor vessel material examinations and compliance with ASME code, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and methods for evaluating transient and accident scenarios, connecting them through the need to ensure that material integrity and examination practices (A) support the ability to meet regulatory requirements for safe cooldown and depressurization during the 24-month refueling cycle (B).

Section A discusses uncertainty analysis methods for transient and accident scenarios, while Section B outlines the process for identifying those accident scenarios, making B a prerequisite for the analyses described in A.

Section A discusses the thermal hydraulic stability and reactor trip mechanisms to prevent SAFDL violations, which are operational safety features that must be compatible with the RCS design described in Section B, specifically the pressurizer and its materials, to ensure safe cooldown and depressurization during a 24-month refueling cycle.
